docs.title: Notes

docs.text: I: Introduction ¶ 1: Employers ¶ Competitive no matter where you are from. Qualifications + skills + experience + application techniques. Want to know: (need to be demonstrate) Your skills Motivation Fit the organization. How long: 5-30 seconds. (make sure it is short and cohesive) 2: CVs ¶ A CV should contain two pages of A4 scale. No need to add any photos. Making changes to each jobs. A written summary. Highlight the reasons. Continue to change as ou acquire new skills. Should be writing for a specific purpose. Read the job description and take some notes: List the Qualifications and Personal attributes. Format: 2 pages (one page for part-time jobs) Use the consistent layout. Text size 11 or 12. Clearing headings and use of bold. Use bullet points instead of paragraph Order: Personal details.(name,tel,email) Education. Working Experience. Relevant: interests or additional skills. (optional) References (depend on the request) Education: Don't forget your degree. Relevant modules Skills from the projects (group works or dissertation) Previous Education. Work Experience Bullet the contents instead of description. Make it relevant. Start with most recent experience. Include the date and title and employer name, location and a brief description of the skills developed. Separate the relevant and additional experience. Additional skills and interests Languages IT skills. Be specify of the interests Attention to details Check your spelling and grammar. name use '_' Check your email address. Format, Order, Relevant, Details 3: Covering letters ¶ The basic: Your CV must be accompanied with a covering letter. Highlight of the CV. Research the employer: Read the job description to find what they are looking for. Company websites, News reports, View updates on social media. layout Letter style One page Dear Sir/Madam: \(\Rightarrow\) Yours faithfully. Dear Mr. : \(\Rrightarrow\) Your sincerely. Structure: Introduction Why they should pick you. Motivation to this company. Additional: Somethings else. Conclude. II: Writing CVs and Making applications ¶ 1: Opening and closing ¶ Find out the name of the person instead of sir or madam. (if possible) Put 'yours sincerely' at the end of the letter with Dear Mr Brown Put 'your faithfully' at the end of the letter with Sir or Madam.

Engineering Mathematics

docs.text: A method of recording hundreds of equations in engineering. (eg. infinite elements with 3D nodes) A matrix is called "lines by column", eg. 2x3 means 2 lines and 3 columns. Capital letter 'A' for a matrix and lower case ' \(a_{ij}\) ' for the element of 'A', 'i' is the line and 'j' is the column. \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix}\) which is a 2x2 matrix.
docs.title: 1.1: Definition and Notation

docs.text: All elements are '0' except the '\' diagonal, but the diagonal can be '0'. e.g, \(\begin{pmatrix}2&0&0\\0&3&0\\0&0&4\end{pmatrix}\)
docs.title: (1): Diagonal Matrix

docs.text: \(\begin{pmatrix}1&1&1\\0&1&1\\0&0&1\end{pmatrix}\) , \(i>j\) , \(a_{ij}=0\) ,which is Upper . \(\begin{pmatrix}1&0&0\\1&1&0\\1&1&1\end{pmatrix}\) , \(i>j\) , \(a_{ij}=0\) , which is Lower .
docs.title: (4): Upper/Lower triangular matrix

docs.text: \(\begin{pmatrix}a&b&c \\ d&e&f\\ g&h&i \end{pmatrix}^T=\begin{pmatrix}a&d&g\\ b&e&h\\ c&f&i \end{pmatrix}\)
docs.title: (1): Transpose means  swap

docs.text: (1): Do the calculation directly. (2): Only same-size matrix can do
docs.title: Scalar multiply

docs.text: Example: \(A=\begin{pmatrix}m&p\\n&q\end{pmatrix},\quad B=\begin{pmatrix}a&c\\b&d\end{pmatrix}\) \(AB=\begin{pmatrix}m&p\\n&q\end{pmatrix}.\begin{pmatrix}a&c\\b&d\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}am+bp&cm+dp\\an+bq&cn+dq\end{pmatrix}\) Notes that \(A.B\not ={B.A}\) (mostly)
docs.title: Matrix Multiplication

docs.text: Example: \(\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\) The 'I' could be 'N x N' matrix \(I.A=A=A.I\) , whilst I is different
docs.title: 1.3: Identity Matrix

docs.text: All the elements are same, \(a_{ij}=b_{ij}\) means '='.
docs.title: (1): Equality

docs.text: Original equations: \(\begin{aligned} 2x-3y=4 \\-x+2y=1\end{aligned}\) Matrix form: \(\begin{pmatrix}2&-3\\-1&2\end{pmatrix} . \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1\end{pmatrix}\)
docs.title: Transfer the Matrix and Simultaneous equations

docs.text: If the matrix \(A=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\) , written \(\det A \left| \begin{array}{cccc}a&b\\ c&d \end{array} \right |=ad-bc\) If \(\det A=0\) , the matrix is singular.
docs.title: (1): Determinant

docs.text: To solve the equation \(\vec{A}X=\vec{b}\) , according to \(A.I=I.A\) , \(X=A^{-1}\vec{b}\) .
docs.title: (2): Inverse of a matrix
1.4: Solve the simulations equation by Gauss's Elimination

docs.text: Firstly, change it to extended matrix form: \(\left[\begin{array}{lcr|r} 1 & -3 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 3 &24 \\ -1 & 2 & 1 &1 \end{array}\right]\) . Use 'add','minus','multiply' and 'divide' to change the left matrix into a upper-triangular matrix. Follow the rule of 'from up to down' and 'from left to right', i.e '2' \(\rightarrow\) '-1' \(\rightarrow\) '2'. Finally got \(\left[\begin{array}{lcr|r}1&-3&1&-9\\ 0&1&2&-8 \\ 0&0&23&-46\end{array}\right]\) , the solution can be solved easily.
docs.title: (1): Elementary Row Operations (ERO)

docs.text: For Example, \(A=\begin{pmatrix}2&3\\-1&4\end{pmatrix}\) . Change \(A\) and \(I\) to extended matrix form, \((A|I)=\left[\begin{array}{lcr|r}2&3&1&0 \\ -1&4&0&1 \end{array}\right]\) . Using EROs changing left-side to \(I\) , the right will be \(A^{-1}\) , \(\left[\begin{array}{lcr|r} 1&0&4/11&-3/11 \\ 0&1&1/11&2/11\end{array}\right]\) . To find the inverse matrix of 2x2 matrix,if \(A=\begin{pmatrix}a&b \\ c&d\end{pmatrix}\) , \(A^{-1}=\frac{1}{|A|}\times A^{*}=\frac{1}{\det A}\times \begin{pmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{pmatrix}\)
docs.title: (2): Finding inverse matrix by EROs

docs.text: For Example, \(\left | \begin{array}{cccc}2&3&-1 \\ 4&1&2 \\ -1&0&3\end{array} \right |=2\left | \begin{array}{cccc}1&2 \\ 0&3 \end{array} \right |-(3)\left |\begin{array}{cccc}4&2 \\ -1&3 \end{array} \right |+(-1)\left | \begin{array}{cccc} 4&1 \\ -1&0 \end{array} \right |=-37\)
docs.title: (3): Determinant of 3x3 matrix

docs.text: Cartesian form in right-hand set are (2,3,1) or \(2\vec{i}+3\vec{j}+\vec{k}\)
docs.title: 2.1: Definition

docs.text: Example: \(W=\vec{F}.\cos\theta\vec{d}=|\vec{F}||\vec{d}|\cos\theta\) , the result is a scalar . \(\vec{i}.\vec{j}=0\) , two vectors are perpendicular. \(\cos\theta=\frac{\vec{a}.\vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}\) Unit vectors: \(\hat{a}=\frac{\vec{a}}{|a|}\) . Component in one direction: \(|\vec{F_1}|=\vec{F}.\hat{a}\) , \(\vec{F_1}=(\vec{F}.\hat{a})\hat{a}\)
docs.title: 2.2: Scalar Product (Dot product)

docs.text: In 2D, \(\vec{M}=|\vec{d}||\vec{F}|\sin\theta\) In 3D, \(\vec{M}=\vec{d}\times\vec{F}=|\vec{d}||\vec{F}|\sin\theta\hat{n}\) Corkscrew rule can justify the direction. \(\vec{m}\times \vec{n}=\left|\begin{array}{cccc}\vec{i}&\vec{j}&\vec{k}\\ a&b&c \\ d&e&f\end{array} \right |=(ae-bd)-(af-dc)+(ae-bd)\)
docs.title: 2.3: Vector Product (Cross Product)

docs.text: \(i^2=-1\) , use 'j' in electrical engineering The coefficient of real number is the real part , the one of imaginary number is the imaginary part . If \(z=a+bi\) , \(\bar{z}=a-bi\) , which is called complex conjugate . \(|z|=|a+bi|=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}\) , which is the modulus of \(z\) .
docs.title: 1: Basic Concepts

docs.text: \(z=x+yi=re^{i\theta}\) , \(\theta=\arg(z)\) , \(r=|z|\) Euler's Identity: \(e^{i\pi}=-1\)
docs.title: (1): Exponential Form / Polar Form

docs.text: \(\cos\theta +i\sin\theta =e^{i\theta}\)
docs.title: (2): Sine/cosine form

docs.text: Try '0',' \(\pm 1\) ' and ' \(\pm 2\) ' to find a real root. Use long division find the roots of the quadratic equation.
docs.title: (3): Complex roots of equations

docs.text: Domain is all the possible input values. Range is all the possible output values.
docs.title: 1.3 Domain and Range

docs.text: We use \(f^{-1}\) to mean inverse. Examples: If \(f(x)=1-2x\) , we write \(f=1-2x\) And we can write it as a function of x : \(f=1-2x\) \(x=\displaystyle\frac{1-f}{2}\) \(f^{-1}(x)=\displaystyle\frac{1-x}{2}\) The graph of the functions and its inverse are reflections of each other in the line \(y=x\) . Domain of \(f(x)\) is equal to the Range of \(f^{-1}\) and vice versa. Other important functions and their inverses: \(e^x\) and \(\ln{x}\) \(\sin x\) and \(sin^{-1} x\)
docs.title: 1.4 Inverse Functions

docs.text: \(e^{-x}\) -the exponential decay \(e^{-x^2}\) -the normal distribution \(xe^{-x}\) -the Poisson distribution
docs.title: 2.1.1 The application of \(e^x\)

docs.text: \(\cosh x=\displaystyle\frac{(e^x+e^{-x})}{2}\) \(\sinh x=\displaystyle\frac{(e^x-e^{-x})}{2}\) Their properties: \((\cosh x)^2-(\sinh x)^2=1\) $$ \displaystyle\frac{d}{dx}\cosh x=\sinh x $$ $$ \displaystyle\frac{d}{dx}\sinh x=\cosh x $$ * \(\cosh (ix)= \cos(x)\) * \(\sinh (ix)= i\sin(x)\)
docs.title: 2.1.2 The hyperbolic cosine and hyperbolic sine

docs.text: The combination of the sine and cosine produce more complicated shapes, such as Fourier Series.
docs.title: 3.1 Combination of sine and cosine functions

docs.text: Even function: \(f(-x)=f(-x)\) Odd function: \(f(-x)=-f(-x)\) Periodic function: like \(\sin x\)
docs.title: 4.4 Odd,even and periodic functions

docs.text: Cross or touch the axes. Max, min and inflection points
docs.title: 5.1 Aims and strategy

docs.text: Using the first derivation to find the gradient each side.
docs.title: 5.2 Stationary points- First derivation

docs.text: The inflection points means the \(\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}\not ={}0\) and \(\displaystyle\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}=0\) . The gradient of the function reach a max or min at the infection points. It is useful only if the \(\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}\not ={0}\)
docs.title: 5.3 Stationary points- Secondary derivation

docs.text: The function that is a quotient of two polynomial functions As the denominator of the fraction takes the value zero, the function becomes infinite, we get a vertical line called vertical asymptote. The function may have horizontal , sloping and vertical asymptote. Theses lines may cross.
docs.title: 6.1 Definitions

docs.text: This is a way to separate the function to make to curves graphing easier. Examples: \(\displaystyle\frac{x^2}{x+1}=\displaystyle\frac{(x-1)(x+1)+1}{x+1}=x-1+\displaystyle\frac{1}{x+1}\) \(\quad\) \(\displaystyle\frac{x+1}{x-3}=\displaystyle\frac{(x-3)+5}{x-3}=1+\displaystyle\frac{5}{x-3}\)
docs.title: 6.2 Rewriting the functions by long division

docs.text: $$ y=\displaystyle\frac{2x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)} $$ * Finding the roots of the denominator, which is the vertical asymptotes. * Finding the monotony of each parts of the function. * Finding the infinite of the function.
docs.title: 7.1: Example 1

docs.text: $$ y=\displaystyle\frac{x^3-2x^2+x-2}{1-x^2} $$ * Separating the factors as \((x-2)(x^2+1)\) . * Following the example 1 to get the vertical asymptotes and the monopoly. * Using the long division to separate the constant to find the slope asymptotes: $$ y=\displaystyle\frac{(1-x^2)(2-x)}{1-x^2} $$ i.e.: $$ y=-x+2+\displaystyle\frac{2x-4}{1-x^2} $$ As the last part of the term is really small, the slope asymptote is the \(y=-x+2\) .
docs.title: 7.2: Example 2

docs.text: \(y=|x+3|+|x-1|\) The graph can be drawn by apart the functions.
docs.title: 7.3: Example 3 (modulus function)
